text: The surgeon places the first and second robotic ports, followed by upsizing the initial five-millimeter entry port to eight millimeters for robotic docking. The areolar tissue in the retro-rectal space is cleared, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon dissects below the arcuate line in the patient's left retro-rectal space, staying within the plane between the retrorectus fat and the transversalis fascia. The robotic ports are docked medial to the linea semilunaris, and this dissection is identified as stage one, focusing on the entire retrorectus space.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath approximately one centimeter lateral to the linea alba, taking care to preserve the integrity of the linea alba for future suture placement during anterior defect closure.

The surgeon navigates the preperitoneal space, aiming to stay between the fatty tissue and the anterior linea alba or diastatic tissue. The approach progresses towards the hernia sac, with an attempt to remain extraperitoneal, though intraperitoneal entry may occur during the ETEP operation.

The surgeon enters the right retrorectus space and incises the posterior rectus sheath approximately one centimeter lateral to the linea alba. Dissection proceeds in the areolar tissue, preserving larger neurovascular bundles, and moves towards the linea semilinaris, with visible landmarks of the linea semilinaris. The dissection continues towards the xiphoid.

The surgeon uses a lateral docking approach to perform a right-sided upper TAR, cutting the right posterior rectus sheath to access the area under the diaphragm. The dissection is completed to ensure mesh overlap under the diaphragm, transitioning from the retro-rectus position to the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon identifies a small defect in the posterior layer and prepares to close it with a running absorbable barb suture. The weakened diastasis, or Swiss cheese defect, extends nearly to the xiphoid. The defect is measured, and the linea alba is re-approximated, incorporating the hernia sac using a number zero absorbable barb suture.

The surgeon completes the re-approximation of the defect in the upper part of the linea alba using robotic image inversion, simulating suturing on the ceiling. The image is reverted, and the space on the floor is measured for mesh placement, ensuring adequate overlap in all directions.

A large piece of medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh, measuring 25 by 34 centimeters, is inserted through an 8mm port without difficulty, maintaining the three-port configuration. The mesh is unrolled and carefully tucked into the retropubic and subdiaphragmatic spaces, then smoothed out as the space is desufflated.
